frameworks.name: encoder-decoder cascade
frameworks.one_line: perception modeled as probabilistic neural population encoding plus a decoding stage

frameworks.name: Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: decoder maximizes likelihood under an assumed encoding distribution

frameworks.name: Fisher information
frameworks.one_line: lower bound on discriminability used to constrain decoder behavior

core_question: When sensory adaptation reshapes the neural encoder, must the decoder also update or can a fixed 'unaware' decoder explain perceptual biases?
key_assumptions: perception arises from an encoder-decoder cascade
adaptation changes the encoder via gain changes in the neural population
decoder either tracks the encoder ('aware') or remains fixed ('unaware')
manipulations: decoder awareness (aware vs unaware of post-adaptation encoder)
sensory attribute tested (motion direction vs contrast)
key_findings: discriminability is bounded from below by the inverse Fisher information for all decoders
estimation bias varies widely across decoders, from zero to substantial
an unaware decoder using the preadaptation encoding likelihood reproduces motion and contrast aftereffect biases
interpretations: adaptation-induced perceptual biases reflect temporary suboptimality of the decoder rather than encoder error
the source of decoder bias is not uniquely identifiable, but unawareness is sufficient to fit behavior
limitations_noted: decoder bias source cannot be uniquely constrained from behavior alone
